CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear logical flow from introduction through method and experiments, and the main contributions (TagLM and empirical gains on CoNLL NER and Chunking) are stated early. Notation and methodological description are consistent and the hierarchical neural tagging + CRF setup is clearly explained; hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is thorough. Figures/tables appear effective and the writing quality is good, though the manuscript would benefit from an explicit discussion of limitations (none are stated) and brief pointers to code or reproducibility to fully aid readers. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear logical flow from introduction through method and experiments, and the main contributions (TagLM and its empirical gains on CoNLL NER and Chunking) are stated early and concretely. Notation and methodological description are consistent and the experimental setup (datasets, LM pretraining corpus, baselines, hyperparameters) is clearly reported, aiding reproducibility. Figures, tables, and examples are effective and the writing quality is good overall, though the paper omits an explicit discussion of limitations and code release, which would improve clarity about practical applicability and replication.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear logical flow from introduction through method and experiments, and the main contributions (TagLM and empirical gains on CoNLL NER and Chunking) are stated early. Notation and methodological description are consistent and the hierarchical neural tagging + CRF setup is clearly explained; hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is thorough. Figures/tables appear effective and the writing quality is good, though the manuscript would benefit from an explicit discussion of limitations (none are stated) and brief pointers to code or reproducibility to fully aid readers.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear logical flow from introduction through method and experiments, and the main contributions (TagLM and its empirical gains on CoNLL NER and Chunking) are stated early and concretely. Notation and methodological description are consistent and the experimental setup (datasets, LM pretraining corpus, baselines, hyperparameters) is clearly reported, aiding reproducibility. Figures, tables, and examples are effective and the writing quality is good overall, though the paper omits an explicit discussion of limitations and code release, which would improve clarity about practical applicability and replication.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notable and practically useful idea—augmenting sequence taggers with pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings (separate forward/backward LMs pretrained on the 1B Word Benchmark and top-layer embeddings concatenated) and shows state-of-the-art gains on CoNLL-2003 NER and CoNLL-2000 Chunking. This is not a wholly novel problem formulation (sequence tagging is well-studied) nor a radical theoretical advance, but the technique is a non-trivial and clearly differentiated extension of prior transfer and joint-learning approaches, yielding substantial empirical improvements (including in low-resource settings). From a theory-first perspective it is somewhat incremental because it treats pretraining empirically without formal analysis, but it would still inform experts by demonstrating the practical value of LM-derived contextual embeddings for tagging. | B(4): The paper presents a notable and practically useful novelty: using separately pretrained forward and backward neural LMs to produce deep token-level embeddings that are concatenated into a supervised sequence tagger (TagLM). While the components (language models, CRF taggers) are established, the specific bidirectional LM embedding augmentation and its clear empirical gains (SOTA on CoNLL 2003 and 2000, strong low-resource improvements) constitute a non-trivial and well-differentiated contribution compared to prior transfer/joint-learning work. This is an incremental but meaningful innovation that would inform practitioners and researchers.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notable and practically useful idea—augmenting sequence taggers with pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings (separate forward/backward LMs pretrained on the 1B Word Benchmark and top-layer embeddings concatenated) and shows state-of-the-art gains on CoNLL-2003 NER and CoNLL-2000 Chunking. This is not a wholly novel problem formulation (sequence tagging is well-studied) nor a radical theoretical advance, but the technique is a non-trivial and clearly differentiated extension of prior transfer and joint-learning approaches, yielding substantial empirical improvements (including in low-resource settings). From a theory-first perspective it is somewhat incremental because it treats pretraining empirically without formal analysis, but it would still inform experts by demonstrating the practical value of LM-derived contextual embeddings for tagging.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notable and practically useful novelty: using separately pretrained forward and backward neural LMs to produce deep token-level embeddings that are concatenated into a supervised sequence tagger (TagLM). While the components (language models, CRF taggers) are established, the specific bidirectional LM embedding augmentation and its clear empirical gains (SOTA on CoNLL 2003 and 2000, strong low-resource improvements) constitute a non-trivial and well-differentiated contribution compared to prior transfer/joint-learning work. This is an incremental but meaningful innovation that would inform practitioners and researchers.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against strong, recent baselines (Chiu & Nichols 2016; Lample et al. 2016; Ma & Hovy 2016; Yang et al. 2017; Hashimoto et al. 2016) on the same standard datasets (CoNLL-2003, CoNLL-2000) using F1, and reports LM pretraining details and perplexities, so comparisons are largely fair. Related work appears thoroughly discussed and the authors claim their gains exceed many prior transfer/joint-learning methods. However, the summary gives no indication of explicit acknowledgement when prior methods are comparable or superior, nor of exhaustive component-level analyses isolating contributions (e.g., forward vs backward LMs, layer choices), so some important comparative and analytical aspects are missing. | B(4): The paper compares against a strong set of recent, appropriate baselines (Chiu & Nichols 2016; Lample et al. 2016; Ma & Hovy 2016; Yang et al. 2017; Hashimoto et al. 2016) on standard benchmarks (CoNLL 2003 NER, CoNLL 2000 Chunking) using the same F1 metric and reports mean scores and hyperparameters. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper claims clear gains (new SOTA) and larger improvements in low-resource settings, which suggests fair empirical framing. Minor concerns remain about reproducibility/code release and explicit statements that prior methods sometimes perform comparably (the summary doesn’t state acknowledgement of comparable results), so I mark this as strong but not perfectly comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against strong, recent baselines (Chiu & Nichols 2016; Lample et al. 2016; Ma & Hovy 2016; Yang et al. 2017; Hashimoto et al. 2016) on the same standard datasets (CoNLL-2003, CoNLL-2000) using F1, and reports LM pretraining details and perplexities, so comparisons are largely fair. Related work appears thoroughly discussed and the authors claim their gains exceed many prior transfer/joint-learning methods. However, the summary gives no indication of explicit acknowledgement when prior methods are comparable or superior, nor of exhaustive component-level analyses isolating contributions (e.g., forward vs backward LMs, layer choices), so some important comparative and analytical aspects are missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against a strong set of recent, appropriate baselines (Chiu & Nichols 2016; Lample et al. 2016; Ma & Hovy 2016; Yang et al. 2017; Hashimoto et al. 2016) on standard benchmarks (CoNLL 2003 NER, CoNLL 2000 Chunking) using the same F1 metric and reports mean scores and hyperparameters. Related work coverage is described as thorough and the paper claims clear gains (new SOTA) and larger improvements in low-resource settings, which suggests fair empirical framing. Minor concerns remain about reproducibility/code release and explicit statements that prior methods sometimes perform comparably (the summary doesn’t state acknowledgement of comparable results), so I mark this as strong but not perfectly comprehensive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the empirical results convincingly support the main claim that bidirectional LM embeddings improve sequence tagging, and the experimental methodology and baselines are broadly sound. Remaining weaknesses are the lack of theoretical analysis, limited dataset scope and ablations, and missing details on code release/statistical testing; these limit generality but do not undermine the core technical correctness, so a score slightly above midpoint is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper's main empirical claim—that augmenting taggers with pretrained bidirectional LM embeddings improves NER and chunking—appears supported by experiments showing state-of-the-art F1 on CoNLL-2003 and CoNLL-2000 and sensible baselines (LM trained on 1B Word). However, from the summary there is no accompanying theoretical analysis or formal explanation for why the LM embeddings should yield these gains, and the experimental scope is narrow (only two sequence‑tagging datasets) with no indication of exhaustive ablations isolating each component (e.g., forward vs backward LMs, layer choice, domain sensitivity). These omissions create logical gaps about when and why the method works and limit confidence in generality, so the paper is technically solid empirically but incomplete from a theory/diagnostic standpoint.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by convincing empirical evidence: bidirectional LM embeddings (TagLM) are pretrained on the 1B Word Benchmark and yield consistent improvements on CoNLL 2003 NER (mean F1 91.93) and CoNLL 2000 Chunking (mean F1 96.37), including substantial gains in low-resource settings. Experimental methodology is broadly sound—strong baselines are compared and hyperparameters are reported—but the summary lacks mention of released code/models and formal statistical significance testing across runs, which would strengthen reproducibility and confidence. No major theoretical errors or unsupported assumptions are apparent from the provided summary, so conclusions are largely justified by results.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses a timely, core problem and delivers clear empirical gains (new state-of-the-art on standard sequence-tagging benchmarks) that make it a strong fit for a top-tier NLP/ML venue. While Reviewer A correctly notes the paper is mainly empirical and lacks theoretical analysis or broader domain evaluation, these limitations are relatively minor with respect to topical appropriateness and do not outweigh the paper’s clear practical contributions and strong empirical results, so I give the highest score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper clearly sits within top-tier NLP/ML scope: it tackles semi-supervised sequence tagging, uses large-scale LM pretraining (1B Word Benchmark), and reports state-of-the-art gains on CoNLL-2003 NER and CoNLL-2000 Chunking with standard metrics. However, from my theory-first perspective the work is primarily empirical with no theoretical justification for why bidirectional LM embeddings should yield the observed gains, and the evaluation is narrowly focused on two related datasets without analyses of failure modes or domain sensitivity. These limitations make it a good but not perfect fit for the highest-tier venues that increasingly expect mechanistic insight or broader empirical validation.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core, timely topic for top NLP/ML venues: semi-supervised sequence tagging using large pretrained language models, and it demonstrates clear empirical gains (new state-of-the-art on CoNLL 2003 NER and CoNLL 2000 Chunking) using LMs pretrained on the 1B Word Benchmark. The methodology and evaluation (F1, perplexity, comparisons to strong prior work such as Lample et al., Ma and Hovy, Yang et al.) match community standards, and the claimed low-resource improvements are practically valuable. My only minor concern is that code availability isn’t mentioned, which affects reproducibility, but that does not reduce the paper’s topical fit for a top-tier venue.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper delivers clear empirical gains with strong baselines, thorough experimental reporting, and useful low-resource analysis, but they also agree it lacks detailed ablations of LM design choices, limited dataset breadth, and discussion of limitations or code release. Given the solid empirical contributions and reproducibility details provided, but acknowledging the missing analyses and broader evaluation that prevent a top mark, a score of 4 appropriately reflects the paper’s substantive value with important but not critical gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments demonstrate clear empirical gains on CoNLL-2003 NER and CoNLL-2000 Chunking using LMs pretrained on the 1B Word Benchmark and report hyperparameters, which supports the core claim. However, the evaluation is narrow (only two sequence-tagging datasets) and the summary does not describe comprehensive ablations or sensitivity analyses isolating key components (e.g., forward vs. backward LM, layer choice, embedding size) that my theory-first standard requires. Related work is said to be thorough, but the paper omits discussion of limitations or failure modes and code availability is not mentioned, limiting reproducibility and understanding of when the method fails or generalizes.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides solid empirical evidence: state-of-the-art results on CoNLL 2003 NER and CoNLL 2000 Chunking, experiments on large unlabeled pretraining (1B Word), and convincing low-resource analyses showing larger gains when labeled data is scarce. It compares to strong baselines and reports hyperparameters, indicating reasonable reproducibility, and related work coverage is thorough. However, the summary omits explicit ablations of LM design choices and layer contributions, lacks discussion of failure cases or limitations, and code/model release is not mentioned — these gaps keep me from a top score.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper delivers clear, reproducible empirical gains (new SOTA on CoNLL-2003 NER and CoNLL-2000 Chunking) and notably large improvements in low-resource settings, indicating a broadly applicable and easy-to-adopt technique. While Reviewer A correctly notes limited theoretical analysis and evaluation breadth, the practical utility, consistent gains, and likely influence on practitioner tooling and follow-up research justify the highest impact score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—improving sequence tagging via large-scale unsupervised pretraining—and demonstrates clear empirical gains including new SOTA on CoNLL-2003 NER and CoNLL-2000 Chunking using bidirectional LM embeddings pretrained on the 1B Word benchmark. From a theory-first perspective the work is impactful in showing the utility of bidirectional LM features (notably large gains in low-resource settings), so it will change practitioner behavior and stimulate follow-up work. However, the contribution is limited by lack of theoretical justification, limited evaluation beyond two datasets, and little analysis of failure modes or domain sensitivity, so it is strong but not transformative.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a central practical problem—improving sequence tagging via semi-supervised learning—and provides convincing, reproducible empirical gains (new SOTA on CoNLL 2003 NER mean F1 91.93 and CoNLL 2000 Chunking mean F1 96.37) by augmenting taggers with bidirectional LM embeddings pretrained on the 1B Word Benchmark. The reported average absolute improvements (≈1.06 F1 for NER, 1.37 F1 for Chunking) and especially the large low-resource boost (3.35 F1 when using 1% of NER data) indicate a broadly applicable, easy-to-integrate technique that will change practitioner tooling and spur follow-up research on LM-based feature transfer.

main_claims: Introducing TagLM: augmenting sequence taggers with pretrained bidirectional language model (LM) embeddings improves performance on sequence tagging tasks.
TagLM establishes new state-of-the-art results on CoNLL 2003 NER (mean F1 91.93) and CoNLL 2000 Chunking (mean F1 96.37) without using additional labeled data or task-specific gazetteers.

LM embeddings yield average absolute improvements of 1.06 F1 for NER and 1.37 F1 for Chunking, and provide larger gains than many prior transfer or joint-learning approaches; benefits are larger in low-resource settings (e.g., 3.35% F1 increase when training on 1% of CoNLL 2003).
_summary.methodology: Pretrain separate forward and backward neural language models on a large unlabeled corpus, extract top-layer forward and backward LM embeddings for each token, concatenate them to form bidirectional LM embeddings, and use these as additional inputs/features in a supervised hierarchical neural sequence tagging model (with a CRF output layer).
baselines: Hierarchical neural tagging baseline used in the paper
_summary.topic_area: NLP - semi-supervised sequence tagging / language-model augmented tagging

reviewer_a.name: Theory-first Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP researcher with a strong background in statistical learning theory and representation learning; holds a PhD in CS and has published theory and model-analysis papers at top venues.
traits: Penalizes papers that provide empirical improvements without accompanying theoretical analysis or formal justification for why the method should work (e.g., no propositions, bounds, or explanatory framework).

Demands comprehensive ablation studies isolating each component (e.g., effect of forward vs backward LM alone, layer choice, embedding dimensionality) and will deduct points if core components are not independently validated.

Downranks papers that treat pretraining as a black box and do not analyze failure modes or conditions under which gains arise (e.g., sensitivity to unlabeled corpus domain mismatch, training data size).
Requires evaluation on at least two diverse datasets (beyond CoNLL-2003 and CoNLL-2000) or strong argument why results generalize; will penalize narrow experimental scope.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on understanding and critiquing the conceptual contribution and internal validity; skeptical of primarily empirical papers unless they provide mechanistic insight and exhaustive ablations.

reviewer_b.name: Empirical NLP Engineer
reviewer_b.background: Applied NLP researcher/engineer with extensive experience building and deploying sequence tagging systems in industry and academia, specializing in transfer learning and pretraining.
traits: Rewards clear, reproducible empirical gains on standard benchmarks (prefers multiple runs with means and standard deviations) and will upscore papers that set new state-of-the-art without extra labeled data.

Values practical contributions that improve low-resource performance and will give credit to methods that are simple to integrate with existing models (e.g., feature/embedding augmentation) and clearly beneficial in realistic settings.
Favors comprehensive comparison to strong baselines (including recent transfer/joint-learning approaches) and will penalize if key competitive baselines are missing or poorly re-implemented.
Prefers that authors release code and pretrained models; will boost score if reproducibility commitments are explicit.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Pragmatic and results-oriented: emphasizes clear empirical benefit, strong baselines, and reproducibility; willing to accept incremental or engineering-style advances if they yield meaningful improvements in practice.
